post.title: Yellow Peas Duty: Will the Trade Grapevine be Proven Right or Wrong After March 31st?
post.excerpt: The March 31st deadline is fast approaching, and in Indian trade circles, the "yellow pea grapevine" is buzzing louder than ever. Since January, speculation has swirled that the current 30% import duty might be scrapped to cool off seasonal price spikes. But...
post.body:
The March 31st deadline is fast approaching, and in Indian trade circles, the "yellow pea grapevine" is buzzing louder than ever. Since January, speculation has swirled that the current 30% import duty might be scrapped to cool off seasonal price spikes. But as we look at the strategic horizon, is this signal or just noise? When we analyze the ground reality, the case for retaining the duty is far more robust than the rumors of its removal. Over the last two years, the government has done an excellent job of walking the tightrope between consumer inflation and farmer welfare. 1. The Mandi Reality Check Domestic arrivals for Tur and Chana are gaining momentum across Indian mandis. However, a significant concern is that prices for both are trading 5-10% below their Minimum Support Price (MSP). Introducing duty-free yellow peas right now would act as a price depressant, hitting farmer income at the exact moment they bring their hard work to market. 2. Safeguarding the Kharif Sowing Season Our farmers aren't just facing market volatility; they are battling a surge in input costs. Geopolitical tensions in the Middle East have pushed up the prices of fuel and fertilizers. If we allow domestic pulse prices to crash now, we drain the purchasing power of farmers just as they prepare for the Kharif season. This could inadvertently discourage Pigeon Pea sowing—the very opposite of our long-term self-sufficiency goals. 3. The Judicial Watch The judiciary has signaled its intent to protect the producer. Only last week, the Supreme Court

asked the government to reconsider the logic of duty-free imports and suggested stakeholder consultations to safeguard pulses farmers. With the next hearing scheduled for May 2026, a sudden shift in policy before consultations would be a significant departure from general norms. 4. A Different Strategic Playbook The 2026 landscape is fundamentally different from 2023. Back then, duty-free yellow peas were a "relief valve" as Pigeon Peas crossed ₹100/kg. Today, the government is better prepared with a 0.6 million ton buffer stock of Pigeon Peas. This allows for surgical interventions to cool retail prices without the "blunt instrument" of imports that could devastate domestic markets. 5. Weak Price Correlation Data shows that over past few months yellow pea CFR prices in USD terms have only seen a moderate 10% rise primarily due to higher freight and insurance cost, while Tur and Urad have jumped over 25% from respective historic lows. This suggests that yellow peas are no longer the "magic bullet" for controlling wider pulse inflation. The Bottom Line: When we weigh the low mandi prices below MSP, the judicial directive, the rising cost of input for the Kharif season, and the availability of specific buffers, the logic is clear. Despite the loud rumors in trade circles, the signals strongly suggest the duty should be retained. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_yellow-peas-duty-will-the-trade- grapevine-activity-7441058405722644480-kGid
post.author: Know Your Commodity
